one_line_takeaway: An intent-aware (Safe/Topical/Toxic) moderation taxonomy plus a LongFormer filter (HarmFormer) and a generation-leak benchmark (HAVOC) that together quantify harmful content in web pretraining data (up to 4.1% toxic in Common Crawl) and in LLM outputs (26.7% leak rate, 76% under provocation).
key_finding: Toxic content is measurable in all three major pretraining corpora (Common Crawl 4.1%, FineWeb 3.9%, C4 2.1%), and keyword-filtered corpora (C4, FineWeb) still retain notable toxic labels (0.45% and 0.29%), showing keyword filtering is insufficient. Topical content is under-preserved in Common Crawl (3.44%) versus C4 (6.81%) and FineWeb (10.63%). On the HAVOC benchmark, aggregating 10,376 generations across six SOTA base LLMs, 26.7% of outputs are harmful (leaks); when given a provocative (toxic) prefix, models leak toxic content 76% of the time, with Sexual harm the most leaked category. TTP and HarmFormer outperform Perspective API and Llama Guard 3 on both the authors' long-form TTP-Eval and the short-form OpenAI Moderation set.
core_contribution: The paper (Microsoft, IJCAI 2025) proposes a three-dimensional content-safety taxonomy (Safe / Topical / Toxic) applied across five harm categories -- Hate & Violence, Ideological Harm, Sexual, Illegal Activities, and Self-Inflicted Harm -- whose central novelty is separating harmful *intent* (Toxic) from socially critical or educational *discourse* (Topical), which prior binary Safe-vs-Toxic moderators conflate. It operationalizes this taxonomy into four released artifacts that are the paper's own contribution (not cited work): (1) TTP-Eval, a 491-webpage expert-annotated long-text moderation benchmark; (2) TTP, a GPT-4-Omni prompt-based classifier; (3) HarmFormer, a LongFormer-based multi-task classifier with five harm heads each doing 3-way Safe/Topical/Toxic classification, built for long-form (1024-token) web pages; and (4) HAVOC, a multi-harm open-ended generation benchmark measuring how much toxic content LLMs 'leak' when prompted with web-derived prefixes.
threat_model: The safety concern is that web-scale pretraining corpora (Common Crawl, C4, FineWeb) contain harmful content that propagates into LLM behavior, and that existing moderation tools (keyword blocklists, HateBERT, Perspective API) fail because they are sentence-level (not long-form), binary (conflating educational discourse with harmful intent), and narrow in harm coverage. The paper frames two risks: (a) toxic pretraining data poisoning model outputs, and (b) LLMs generating actionable harmful content (e.g., self-harm methods) when given provocative or even seemingly benign inputs -- HAVOC operationalizes the latter as 'leaks'.
limitations: The authors state the study is English-only (multilingual left to future work) and the taxonomy ignores culture-specific sensitivities. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: HarmFormer's train/test labels are auto-generated by TTP (GPT-4-Omni), so HarmFormer's reported 85% F1 measures agreement with the TTP labeler rather than independent ground truth; only the 491-page TTP-Eval has human labels. Per-harm quality is weak on Hate & Violence (HarmFormer F1 0.51, TTP F1 0.67), driven by 'reporting' webpages that neutrally report incidents being confused with promotion. HAVOC leak labeling itself relies on TTP, so the 26.7% figure inherits TTP's error profile. The prevalence percentages are TTP-estimated, not human-verified at scale.

datasets_used: Common Crawl (1M pages audited; source of TTP-Eval candidate pool)
TTP-Eval (own, 491 expert-annotated web pages; Krippendorff Alpha 0.77)
HarmFormer training corpus (own, 258K TTP-labeled pages; 253K train / 14K dev / 14K test)
OpenAI Moderation test set (1158 non-toxic + 522 toxic sentences)
RealToxicityPrompts (RTP) used for comparison against HAVOC
quantitative_results: Prevalence of Toxic content: Common Crawl 4.1%, C4 2.1%, FineWeb 3.9%
Prevalence of Topical content: Common Crawl 3.44%, C4 6.81%, FineWeb 10.63%
Residual Toxic labels in keyword-filtered sets: C4 0.45%, FineWeb 0.29%
TTP on TTP-Eval toxic dimension: Precision 0.87, Recall 0.79, F1 0.83
HarmFormer (LongFormer, 1024 ctx) on test set aggregated toxic: Precision 0.88, Recall 0.81, F1 0.85
HarmFormer per-harm F1: Sexual 0.91, Self-Inflicted 0.88, Illegal 0.76, Ideological 0.61, Hate & Violence 0.51
LongFormer 1024 vs 512 context: +2 F1 (0.85 vs 0.83); XLM-Roberta FT 0.82; HateBERT FT 0.81
OpenAI Moderation set: TTP F1 0.80 (P0.72/R0.91), HarmFormer F1 0.73 (P0.64/R0.84), Perspective API F1 0.66, Llama Guard 3 Few-Shot F1 0.70
HAVOC overall leak rate: 26.7% (26.76); Provocative 76.23%, Passive 12.31%, Neutral 9.64%
Sexual toxic content composition: Common Crawl 78% explicit pornography vs 52% subtler patterns in C4/FineWeb
baselines_compared: Perspective API (F1 0.63 on TTP-Eval; 0.66 on OpenAI Moderation)
Llama Guard 3 (focused/zero-shot/few-shot; F1 0.66/0.68/0.70 on OpenAI Moderation)
HateBERT and XLM-Roberta as HarmFormer base-model ablations
TTP prompt ported to DeepSeek-R1 (LLaMa 32B) F1 0.06, Gemini 2 Flash F1 0.64, Gemma 2 27B F1 0.35 (vs GPT-4-Omni TTP 0.83)
RealToxicityPrompts (RTP) as the prior generation benchmark HAVOC is contrasted against
